name: linkedin_ai_writing_filter
goal: Reduce AI-detectable patterns in LinkedIn drafts by enforcing specificity, grounded evidence, natural voice, and non-templated structure.

lint_rules.description: Flags vague quantifiers that make the post sound universally applicable.

instructions: Replace generic quantifiers with a scoped context (role type, hiring volume, geography, process stage, ATS, timeline).
If you cannot add a real constraint, qualify the claim (e.g., 'in high-volume roles', 'in our process', 'in my experience').

lint_rules.description: Flags 'It's the X. The Y. The Z.' and similar rhythmic triplets.

instructions: Collapse into 1 to 2 sentences with uneven length and one concrete example.
Add one 'messy' real-world detail (timing, system limitation, handoff confusion, common candidate question).

lint_rules.description: Flags precise stats without a source line.

instructions: Either add a source line with publication name and year (no link needed), or remove the percentage.
If removing, replace with a bounded, non-numeric claim (e.g., 'A lot of candidates', 'It consistently shows up in feedback').

lint_rules.description: Flags sudden product-category reveals like 'That’s where X changes the equation'.

instructions: Replace buzzword term with plain language first, then optionally name the category once.
Add one limitation or boundary (what it does not do, what it escalates, what it can’t answer).

lint_rules.description: Flags long sequences of benefits with no constraints, risks, or implementation notes.

instructions: Cut the benefit list by 30 to 50 percent.
Add at least one boundary: escalation rule, knowledge limits, compliance note, integration requirement, or 'when it shouldn't answer'.

lint_rules.description: Flags standalone slogan lines that read like a generated 'mic drop'.
patterns: ^And that opinion is your employer brand\.$
^Your employer brand isn't built in the offer letter\..+$

instructions: Keep at most one slogan-like line in the whole post.
If keeping one, immediately follow it with a concrete example or observation.

lint_rules.description: Flags overly consistent short line breaks and uniform sentence length.

instructions: Merge some lines into fuller sentences, then add one shorter line for emphasis.
Allow one paragraph to be longer and more detailed.

lint_rules.description: Flags repeated em dash usage as a stylistic crutch.

instructions: Replace extra em dashes with a period or comma.
Only keep one em dash if it is essential to clarity.

lint_rules.description: Flags absence of personal perspective or concrete observation.

instructions: Add one authorship marker: 'I reviewed...', 'We changed...', 'A candidate asked...', 'In our ATS...'.
Include one concrete detail: timeframe (24h, 3 days), system name, stage name, or a real candidate question.
Avoid inventing client names. Use anonymised but specific detail.
Do not follow the full canonical LinkedIn formula end-to-end.
Either start with a concrete observation or open with a mini-story.
If you use a question at the end, ensure it is specific and not generic.

prefer_phrases: what I saw in the last audit
one pattern that keeps showing up
this is where it helps, and where it doesn't

examples: A confirmation email that doesn't include timeline, next step, or who to contact.
A 5-day gap between application and first update for warehouse roles.
Candidates asking 'Is salary range set?' because the posting is vague.

post_quality_checks.definition: A standalone line that reads like a universal truth and could be pasted into any post.

instructions: Replace the opening generalization with a concrete moment and context.
Use one detail: role type, timeline, or system artifact (ATS email, careers page section).
Add a boundary sentence: escalation to recruiter, what it refuses to answer, or governance requirement.
